title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: prediction interval claims 95% coverage actually covers true value 70% time worse useless — gives decision-makers false confidence. genomic surveillance, can mean underestimating probability variant reaches critical threshold, leading delayed public health response. Calibration asks simple question: model says “95% chance true frequency lies interval,” observation actually fall within interval 95% time? Surprisingly surveillance forecasting tools assess systematically. lineagefreq provides three capabilities forecast calibration , knowledge, genomic surveillance package offers: calibration diagnostics (calibrate()), post-hoc recalibration (recalibrate()), distribution-free conformal prediction intervals (conformal_forecast()).

what: PIT diagnostics on real CDC data
title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: Probability Integral Transform (PIT) provides comprehensive calibration diagnostic. perfectly calibrated forecaster, PIT values uniformly distributed [0, 1]. Departures uniformity reveal specific miscalibration patterns: U-shaped PIT histogram indicates underdispersion (intervals narrow), hump shape indicates overdispersion (intervals wide). reliability diagram plots observed coverage nominal coverage levels 10% 90%. Perfect calibration lies diagonal. Points diagonal indicate overconfidence (model claims narrower intervals warranted); points indicate conservatism.
code: library(lineagefreq) data(cdc_ba2_transition)  x <- lfq_data(cdc_ba2_transition, lineage = lineage,               date = date, count = count) bt <- backtest(x, engines = "mlr",                horizons = c(7, 14, 21, 28), min_train = 42)  cal <- calibrate(bt) cal  # PIT histogram plot(cal, type = "pit")  # Reliability diagram plot(cal, type = "reliability")

title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: PIT diagnostic reveals miscalibration, recalibrate() adjusts prediction intervals improve coverage. Two methods available: Isotonic regression learns nonparametric, monotone mapping nominal observed coverage using backtest data inverts . requires distributional assumptions. Platt scaling fits logistic regression relationship standardised residuals coverage, providing smooth parametric adjustment.
code: fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr") fc  <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28)  # Isotonic recalibration fc_recal <- recalibrate(fc, bt, method = "isotonic") autoplot(fc_recal)

title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: Parametric prediction intervals forecast() assume multinomial logistic model correctly specified parameter estimation uncertainty well captured Fisher information matrix. assumptions violated — may rapid variant replacement waves — intervals can substantially miscalibrated. Conformal prediction offers distribution-free alternative. Split conformal inference (Vovk et al. 2005) provides prediction intervals exact finite-sample coverage guarantees assumption exchangeability: conformal intervals typically wider parametric intervals model well-specified, narrower model misspecified — precisely situation accurate uncertainty quantification matters .
code: fc_conf <- conformal_forecast(fit, x, horizon = 28,                               ci_level = 0.95, seed = 42) autoplot(fc_conf)

title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: time-series data distribution shifts time (variant replacement wave), exchangeability assumption violated. Adaptive conformal inference (ACI; Gibbs Candes, 2021) addresses adjusting miscoverage level online: gamma parameter controls learning rate: larger values track distribution shifts aggressively increase interval variability.
code: fc_aci <- conformal_forecast(fit, x, horizon = 28,                              method = "aci", gamma = 0.05)

title: Prediction Calibration and Conformal Inference
text: score_forecasts() now supports four additional proper scoring rules alongside original MAE, RMSE, coverage, WIS: CRPS (Continuous Ranked Probability Score): integral squared difference forecast CDF empirical CDF observation. Simultaneously rewards calibration sharpness. Log score: negative log-likelihood observation forecast distribution. Heavily penalises confident wrong forecasts. DSS (Dawid-Sebastiani Score): depends first two moments, providing computationally efficient alternative log score. Calibration score: mean squared calibration error across nominal levels 10%–90%. scoring rules proper sense Gneiting Raftery (2007): minimised expectation forecast distribution equals true data-generating distribution. means forecaster improve expected score issuing dishonest predictions.
code: sc <- score_forecasts(bt, metrics = c("mae", "crps", "log_score",                                       "dss", "calibration")) compare_models(sc)

what: The confounding of transmissibility and immune escape
title: Immune-Aware Fitness Estimation
text: Standard multinomial logistic regression estimates single growth rate per lineage. observed growth advantage new variant conflates two distinct mechanisms: intrinsic transmissibility (variant’s ability spread naive population) immune escape (ability evade existing population immunity). variant moderate intrinsic transmissibility strong immune escape can observed growth rate one high intrinsic transmissibility escape . public health implications two scenarios quite different. lineagefreq v0.4.0 introduces tools disentangling components, incorporating external immunological data, computing early warning signals genomic data alone.

title: Immune-Aware Fitness Estimation
text: first step encoding known population-level immunity circulating lineage. may come seroprevalence surveys, vaccination coverage data, model-based reconstructions. immunity values represent proportion population sufficient neutralising antibody titres prevent infection lineage. necessarily approximate — surveillance system measures directly — even rough estimates improve decomposition substantially ignoring immunity entirely.
code: library(lineagefreq)  # Immunity data: proportion of population with neutralising # protection against each lineage at each time point imm_data <- data.frame(   date = rep(seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), by = "week",                  length.out = 15), each = 3),   lineage = rep(c("JN.1", "XBB.1.5", "BA.5"), 15),   immunity = c(     rep(c(0.10, 0.55, 0.65), 8),     rep(c(0.25, 0.50, 0.60), 7)   ) )  il <- immune_landscape(imm_data, date = date,                        lineage = lineage, immunity = immunity) plot(il)

title: Immune-Aware Fitness Estimation
text: Given fitted frequency model immunity landscape, fitness_decomposition() partitions lineage’s growth advantage intrinsic escape components: decomposition follows framework Figgins Bedford (2025). lineage, effective fitness modelled product intrinsic transmissibility susceptible fraction population. escape component differential susceptibility log space: variant facing lower population immunity fitness advantage escape, regardless intrinsic transmissibility.
code: data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1) x <- lfq_data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1, lineage = lineage,               date = date, count = count) fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr")  fd <- fitness_decomposition(fit, il, generation_time = 5) fd plot(fd)

title: Immune-Aware Fitness Estimation
text: new variant first detected, sequence counts sparse reliable growth rate estimation. Deep Mutational Scanning (DMS) data — laboratory measurements individual mutations affect receptor binding antibody escape — can serve informative prior. fit_dms_prior() implements penalised multinomial logistic regression shrinks growth rate estimates toward DMS-derived priors: lambda parameter controls strength prior. lambda = 0, result identical standard MLR. lambda increases, estimates pulled toward DMS prior. appropriate value depends relative informativeness DMS data quantity observed sequences — judgment call informed cross-validation calibration tools previous vignette.
code: # DMS escape scores from Bloom Lab measurements dms <- c("JN.1" = 0.05, "XBB.1.5" = 0.01, "BA.5" = -0.02)  fit_dms <- fit_dms_prior(x, dms_scores = dms, lambda = 3) growth_advantage(fit_dms)

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: lineagefreq provides multiple modeling engines unified fit_model() interface. vignette shows compare using built-backtesting framework. Three engines available v0.1.0: mlr: Multinomial logistic regression (frequentist, fast). piantham: Converts MLR growth rates relative reproduction numbers using generation-time approximation. hier_mlr: Hierarchical MLR partial pooling across locations via empirical Bayes shrinkage.

title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: Genomic surveillance programmes operate fixed budgets. Laboratories finite sequencing capacity, capacity must distributed across regions, time periods, sampling strategies. Static allocation rules (equal per region, proportional population) ignore information content additional sequence. Optimal allocation requires knowing next sequence reduce uncertainty . lineagefreq v0.5.0 introduces five functions address problem information-theoretic decision-theoretic perspective.

title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: surveillance_value() quantifies marginal decision value sequencing additional samples, given current posterior uncertainty variant frequency estimates: EVOI curve shows diminishing returns: first 50 additional sequences reduce estimation variance substantially, next 50 contribute less. informs budget decisions — point, additional sequencing yields negligible improvement frequency estimates, resources better directed elsewhere.
code: library(lineagefreq) sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 4, n_timepoints = 15,   advantages = c("A" = 1.4, "B" = 1.1, "C" = 0.8), seed = 1) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr")  ev <- surveillance_value(fit, n_current = 500) ev plot(ev)

title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: adaptive_design() goes beyond static allocation reallocating resources across regions surveillance round, directing effort toward strata uncertainty reduction highest decision value: Thompson sampling naturally balances exploration (sampling uncertain regions) exploitation (sampling variants prevalent). alternative UCB strategy provides deterministic upper confidence bound approach. contrasts static Neyman allocation implemented survinger package, optimises single time point. Adaptive allocation responds evolving variant landscape better suited multi-round surveillance campaigns.
code: ad <- adaptive_design(sim, capacity = 200, n_rounds = 10,   strategy = "thompson", seed = 42) ad plot(ad)

title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: detection_horizon() answers prospective question: given current sequencing capacity hypothetical new variant initial prevalence p0p_0 growing rate rr, many weeks reliable detection? function accounts multinomial sampling logistic growth computes cumulative detection probability across surveillance periods. extends sequencing_power(), answers static question (many sequences single period), adding temporal dimension.
code: dh <- detection_horizon(initial_prev = 0.001, growth_rate = 1.3,   n_per_period = 300, confidence = 0.95) dh wtd <- attr(dh, "weeks_to_detection")

what: Sequential detection with controlled false alarms
title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: alert_threshold() implements sequential testing emerging variants, controlling false alarm rate minimising detection delay: Two methods available: SPRT (Wald, 1945): Accumulates log-likelihood ratios null (stable frequency) alternative (growing). Stops evidence crosses either boundary. Controls false alarm (α\alpha) missed detection (β\beta) rates. CUSUM: Accumulates positive deviations expected frequency. Simpler implement control missed detection probability explicitly. SPRT particularly suited surveillance settings false alarms costly (triggering unnecessary public health responses) delayed detection also costly (missing emerging variant concern).
code: alerts <- alert_threshold(sim, method = "sprt",   alpha = 0.05, delta_1 = 0.03) alerts

title: Information-Theoretic Surveillance Optimization
text: surveillance_dashboard() combines current landscape, growth advantages, detection power, calibration diagnostics single multi-panel display weekly reporting: dashboard designed programme managers, statisticians. panel answers specific operational question: circulating? fast growing? Can detect rare variants? forecasts reliable?
code: bt <- backtest(sim, engines = "mlr", horizons = c(7, 14),   min_train = 42) panels <- surveillance_dashboard(fit, sim, bt = bt)

title: Validation Report
text: vignette documents quantitative validation lineagefreq real CDC SARS-CoV-2 surveillance data published benchmark results Abousamra, Figgins, Bedford (2024). results fully reproducible using datasets shipped package.

title: Validation Report
text: evaluate MLR engine CDC BA.1--BA.2 transition dataset (December 2021 June 2022, 150 observations across 5 lineages) using rolling-origin backtesting 42-day minimum training window.
code: library(lineagefreq) data(cdc_ba2_transition) x <- lfq_data(cdc_ba2_transition, lineage = lineage,               date = date, count = count) bt <- backtest(x, engines = "mlr",                horizons = c(7, 14, 21, 28), min_train = 42) sc <- score_forecasts(bt, metrics = c("mae", "crps", "coverage"))

title: Validation Report
text: Abousamra et al. (2024, PLOS Computational Biology) reported MLR forecast accuracy multi-country evaluation: point estimates consistent published results. slightly higher MAE 28 days may reflect specific BA.2 transition dynamics (four sequential subvariant sweeps occurring simultaneously), particularly challenging forecasting scenario.

title: Validation Report
text: forecast accuracy numbers obscure critical issue: 95 percent prediction intervals severely miscalibrated.
code: cal <- calibrate(bt) cal plot(cal, type = "reliability") plot(cal, type = "pit")

title: Validation Report
text: PIT histogram shows strong U-shape (KS test D = 0.41, p < 0.001), indicating systematic underdispersion: MLR prediction intervals narrow. nominal 90 percent coverage, 46 percent observations fall within intervals. mean absolute calibration error 0.24 — substantially worse 0.05 threshold indicate adequate calibration. finding invisible standard MAE-based evaluation. forecaster reporting MAE = 3.4 pp 7 days appear highly accurate, prediction intervals — drive decision-making uncertainty — unreliable. precisely calibration diagnostics essential.

title: Validation Report
text: Isotonic regression recalibration widens intervals match empirical coverage:
code: fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr") fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28) fc_recal <- recalibrate(fc, bt, method = "isotonic")

title: Validation Report
text: Conformal prediction intervals provide distribution-free coverage guarantees. BA.2 data, conformal intervals substantially wider parametric intervals (mean width 0.59 vs 0.07), correctly reflecting actual estimation uncertainty parametric intervals understate.
code: fc_conf <- conformal_forecast(fit, x, horizon = 28,   ci_level = 0.95, seed = 42)

title: Validation Report
text: apply SPRT-based alerting JN.1 emergence dataset (October 2023 June 2024). JN.1 crossed 5 percent national frequency around November 25, 2023.
code: data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1) x_jn1 <- lfq_data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1, lineage = lineage,                    date = date, count = count) alerts <- alert_threshold(x_jn1, method = "sprt",   delta_1 = 0.02, alpha = 0.05)

title: Validation Report
text: SPRT trigger alert JN.1 biweekly data. reflects real limitation: 2–3 observations JN.1’s first detection crossing 5 percent threshold, sequential test accumulate sufficient evidence. Weekly frequent surveillance data provide observations per unit time improve detection sensitivity. SPRT best suited high-frequency monitoring; biweekly monthly data, non-sequential summarize_emerging() function using binomial trend tests appropriate.

title: Validation Report
text: validate decomposition simulated data known ground truth. intrinsic transmissibility immune escape specified separately, decomposition recovers components sum exactly observed growth rate (residual < 1e-15). decomposition mathematically exact (intrinsic + escape = observed advantage). However, decomposition requires external immunity data — frequency dynamics alone identify two components. identifiability constraint enforced software: fitness_decomposition() requires immune_landscape object input error without one.
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.3, "B" = 0.9),   n_timepoints = 20, total_per_tp = 1000, seed = 42) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr") # ... construct immunity landscape and decompose ...

title: Adaptive sequencing allocation via Thompson sampling — adaptive_design
text: data lfq_data object location column. capacity Total sequencing capacity per round (integer). n_rounds Number allocation rounds simulate. Default NULL uses number time points data. strategy Allocation strategy: "thompson" (default) Thompson sampling, "ucb" Upper Confidence Bound. target_lineage Character; lineage optimise detection . Default NULL optimises overall frequency estimation. exploration Exploration parameter UCB. Default 2.0. Larger values explore ; smaller values exploit current best regions. seed Random seed. Default NULL.

title: Adaptive sequencing allocation via Thompson sampling — adaptive_design
text: adaptive_allocation S3 class components: allocations Tibble round, region, n_allocated, uncertainty, frequency. summary Tibble per-region totals mean allocation. strategy Character; strategy used. capacity Integer; per-round capacity.

title: Adaptive sequencing allocation via Thompson sampling — adaptive_design
text: Thompson sampling draws posterior distribution frequency estimates allocates proportional posterior variance. Regions high uncertainty receive sequences, stochastic draws naturally balance exploration (sampling uncertain regions) exploitation (sampling variants prevalent). UCB allocates proportional upper confidence bound estimation error: \(\text{score}_r = \hat{\sigma}_r + c \sqrt{2 \log(t) / n_r}\) \(\hat{\sigma}_r\) current estimation uncertainty region \(r\), \(n_r\) cumulative allocation, \(t\) round, \(c\) exploration parameter.

what: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
text: Applies sequential probability ratio test (SPRT) CUSUM procedure lineage frequency data, determining accumulated evidence sufficient declare variant "emerging" rather sampling noise. Controls false alarm rate minimising detection delay.

title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
code: alert_threshold(   data,   method = c("sprt", "cusum"),   alpha = 0.05,   beta = 0.1,   delta_0 = 0,   delta_1 = 0.03,   threshold = 5 )

title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
text: data lfq_data object. method Detection method: "sprt" (default) sequential probability ratio test, "cusum" cumulative sum control chart. alpha False alarm probability. Default 0.05. beta Missed detection probability. Default 0.10. delta_0 Null hypothesis growth rate (emergence). Default 0 (frequency stable). delta_1 Alternative hypothesis growth rate (emergence). Default 0.03 (3 percent per-week increase logit scale). threshold CUSUM decision threshold. Default 5.0. used method = "cusum".

title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
text: tibble columns lineage, date, statistic (log-likelihood ratio CUSUM value), alert (logical), direction (emerging/declining/ stable), confidence (1 - alpha).

title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
text: SPRT (Wald, 1945) computes log-likelihood ratio alternative (lineage growing rate \(\delta_1\)) null (frequency stable). test stops cumulative log-ratio crosses upper boundary \(B = \log((1-\beta)/\alpha)\) (declare emerging) lower boundary \(= \log(\beta/(1-\alpha))\) (declare stable). CUSUM accumulates deviations expected frequency null: \(S_t = \max(0, S_{t-1} + (x_t - k))\) \(x_t\) observed frequency change \(k\) allowance (half shift detect). alert raised \(S_t > h\).

title: Sequential detection of emerging variants — alert_threshold
text: Wald (1945). Sequential tests statistical hypotheses. Annals Mathematical Statistics, 16(2), 117–186.

text: Assesses whether prediction intervals backtesting well-calibrated computing PIT (Probability Integral Transform) values, reliability diagrams, uniformity tests. perfectly calibrated forecaster produces PIT values uniformly distributed \([0, 1]\).

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
code: calibrate(x, observed = NULL, n_bins = 10L)  # S3 method for class 'lfq_backtest' calibrate(x, observed = NULL, n_bins = 10L)  # S3 method for class 'lfq_forecast' calibrate(x, observed = NULL, n_bins = 10L)

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
text: x lfq_backtest object backtest, lfq_forecast object forecast together observed data. observed lfq_forecast objects: numeric vector observed frequencies corresponding forecast rows. Ignored lfq_backtest objects (carry observed values). n_bins Number bins PIT histogram reliability diagram. Default 10.

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
text: calibration_report object (S3 class) components: pit_values Numeric vector PIT values \([0,1]\). pit_histogram Tibble bin, count, density, expected columns. reliability Tibble nominal, observed coverage level. ks_test List statistic p_value Kolmogorov-Smirnov test uniformity. n Integer; number forecast-observation pairs used.

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
text: PIT value single forecast-observation pair defined quantile observation within forecast distribution. assumption Gaussian prediction intervals (parametric simulation forecast approximates), PIT computed \(\Phi((y - \hat{y}) / \hat{\sigma})\) \(\hat{y}\) predicted median, \(\hat{\sigma}\) derived prediction interval width, \(\Phi\) standard normal CDF. reliability diagram plots observed coverage nominal coverage levels 10 90 percent. Perfect calibration lies diagonal.

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
text: Gneiting T, Balabdaoui F, Raftery AE (2007). Probabilistic forecasts, calibration sharpness. Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 69(2), 243–268. doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2007.00587.x

title: Calibration diagnostics for lineage frequency forecasts — calibrate
code: # \donttest{ sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8),   n_timepoints = 20, seed = 1) bt <- backtest(sim, engines = "mlr",   horizons = c(7, 14), min_train = 42) cal <- calibrate(bt) #> Warning: ties should not be present for the one-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test cal #>  #> ── Calibration report  #> 75 forecast-observation pairs #> KS test for PIT uniformity: D = 0.2464, p = 0.000222 #> Mean absolute calibration error: 0.263 # }

text: fit lfq_fit object (engine). data lfq_data object used fit model. horizon Number days forecast. Default 28. ci_level Target coverage level. Default 0.95. method Conformal method: "split" (default) split conformal prediction, "aci" adaptive conformal inference online coverage correction. cal_fraction Fraction data reserved calibration set (split conformal ). Default 0.3. gamma Learning rate adaptive conformal inference. Default 0.05. Controls quickly coverage target adjusts response observed miscoverage. seed Random seed calibration split. Default NULL.

title: Conformal prediction intervals for lineage frequencies — conformal_forecast
text: lfq_forecast object conformal prediction intervals. object fully compatible autoplot forecast methods.

title: Conformal prediction intervals for lineage frequencies — conformal_forecast
text: Split conformal prediction partitions training data proper training set calibration set. model refit training set, conformity scores (absolute residuals) computed calibration set. prediction interval new point point forecast plus minus \((1 - \alpha)(1 + 1/n_{\text{cal}})\) quantile calibration scores. provides exact \(1 - \alpha\) marginal coverage exchangeability (Vovk et al. 2005). Adaptive conformal inference (ACI) (Gibbs Candes, 2021) adjusts miscoverage level online maintain long-run coverage even data distribution shifts time, typical surveillance data variant replacement waves.

title: Conformal prediction intervals for lineage frequencies — conformal_forecast
text: Vovk V, Gammerman , Shafer G (2005). Algorithmic Learning Random World. Springer. Gibbs , Candes E (2021). Adaptive conformal inference distribution shift. Advances Neural Information Processing Systems, 34.

what: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon
title: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon
text: Given current sequencing capacity hypothetical variant initial prevalence \(p_0\) growing rate \(\delta\), estimates number surveillance periods (weeks) probability detection exceeds specified threshold.

title: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon
text: initial_prev Initial prevalence emerging variant. Default 0.001 (0.1 percent). growth_rate Per-week multiplicative growth rate frequency scale. Default 1.3 (30 percent per week). n_per_period Sequences collected per surveillance period. Default 500. n_periods Maximum number periods evaluate. Default 26 (6 months weekly data). detection_threshold Minimum number sequences target lineage required declare detection. Default 1. confidence Detection probability threshold. Default 0.95.

title: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon
text: tibble columns period, prevalence, detection_prob (cumulative detection probability), detected (logical). attribute weeks_to_detection contains first period detection probability exceeds confidence threshold, NA reached.

title: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon
text: period \(t\), variant prevalence modelled logistic growth: \(p(t) = p_0 \cdot r^t / (1 - p_0 + p_0 \cdot r^t)\) \(r\) per-period growth rate. probability detecting least \(k\) sequences \(n\) draws prevalence \(p\) \(1 - F_{\text{Binom}}(k-1; n, p)\). Cumulative detection \(1 - \prod_{\tau=1}^{t}(1 - P_\tau)\).

title: Detection horizon for an emerging variant — detection_horizon

what: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
text: penalised multinomial logistic regression engine incorporates Deep Mutational Scanning (DMS) escape scores informative priors variant fitness. valuable early-emergence scenarios new lineage observed sequences laboratory-measured phenotypic data (e.g., ACE2 binding affinity, antibody escape) available.

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
code: fit_dms_prior(data, dms_scores, lambda = 1, pivot = NULL, ci_level = 0.95)

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
text: data lfq_data object. dms_scores Named numeric vector DMS-derived fitness priors. Names correspond lineage identifiers. Values log growth rate scale (positive = fitter average). Lineages vector receive prior 0. lambda Regularisation strength (penalty weight). Default 1.0. Larger values pull estimates strongly toward DMS prior. lambda = 0, result identical standard MLR engine. pivot Reference lineage name. Default NULL (automatic selection). ci_level Confidence level. Default 0.95.

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
text: lfq_fit object compatible downstream functions (forecast, growth_advantage, etc.).

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
text: approach uses penalised maximum likelihood penalty proportional squared difference estimated growth rate DMS-derived prior. implements empirical Bayes shrinkage: abundant data, penalty little effect; sparse data, estimates pulled toward DMS prior. penalised log-likelihood : $$\ell_{\text{pen}}(\alpha, \delta) = \ell(\alpha, \delta) - \frac{\lambda}{2} \sum_v (\delta_v - \mu_v)^2$$ \(\ell\) standard multinomial log-likelihood, \(\delta_v\) growth rate lineage \(v\), \(\mu_v\) DMS prior. Hessian adjusted accordingly, ensuring correct confidence interval widths.

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior
text: Dadonaite B, Crawford KHD, Radford CE, et al. (2023). pseudovirus system enables deep mutational scanning full SARS-CoV-2 spike. Cell, 186(6), 1263–1278. doi:10.1016/j.cell.2023.02.001 Bloom JD, Neher RA (2023). Fitness effects mutations SARS-CoV-2 proteins. Virus Evolution, 9(2), vead055. doi:10.1093/ve/vead055

title: Fit model with Deep Mutational Scanning priors — fit_dms_prior

what: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: Unified interface modeling lineage frequency dynamics. Supports multiple engines share input/output contract.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
code: fit_model(   data,   engine = c("mlr", "hier_mlr", "piantham", "fga", "garw"),   pivot = NULL,   horizon = 28L,   ci_level = 0.95,   ... )

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: data lfq_data object. engine Model engine use: "mlr" (default): Multinomial logistic regression. Fast, frequentist, external dependencies. "hier_mlr": Hierarchical MLR partial pooling across locations. Requires .location column data. "piantham": Piantham approximation converting MLR growth rates relative reproduction numbers. Requires generation_time argument. "fga": Fixed growth advantage model (Bayesian via CmdStan). Requires 'CmdStan'; check lfq_stan_available(). "garw": Growth advantage random walk model (Bayesian via CmdStan). Allows fitness change time. pivot Reference lineage name. Growth rates reported relative lineage (fixed 0). Default: lineage highest count earliest time point. horizon Forecast horizon days (stored later use forecast()). Default 28. ci_level Confidence level intervals. Default 0.95. ... Engine-specific arguments passed internal engine function. engine = "mlr": window, ci_method, laplace_smooth. engine = "piantham": generation_time (required). engine = "hier_mlr": shrinkage_method.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: lfq_fit object (S3 class), list containing: engine Engine name (character). growth_rates Named numeric vector growth rates per time_scale days (pivot = 0). intercepts Named numeric vector intercepts. pivot Name pivot lineage. lineages Character vector lineage names. fitted_values Tibble fitted frequencies. residuals Tibble observed, fitted, Pearson residuals. vcov_matrix Variance-covariance matrix. loglik, aic, bic Model fit statistics. nobs, n_timepoints, df Sample model sizes. ci_level, horizon specified. call matched call.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: MLR engine models frequency lineage \(v\) time \(t\) : $$p_v(t) = \frac{\exp(\alpha_v + \delta_v t)}{\sum_k \exp(\alpha_k + \delta_k t)}$$ \(\alpha_v\) intercept, \(\delta_v\) growth rate per time_scale days (default 7), pivot lineage \(\alpha = \delta = 0\). Parameters estimated maximum likelihood via optim(method = "BFGS") Hessian used asymptotic Wald confidence intervals. constant growth rate assumption appropriate monotonic variant replacement periods (typically 2–4 months). longer periods non-monotonic dynamics, use window argument restrict estimation window, consider "garw" engine allows time-varying growth advantages.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: Abousamra E, Figgins M, Bedford T (2024). Fitness models provide accurate short-term forecasts SARS-CoV-2 variant frequency. PLoS Computational Biology, 20(9):e1012443. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012443 Piantham C, Linton NM, Nishiura H (2022). Predicting trajectory replacements SARS-CoV-2 variants using relative reproduction numbers. Viruses, 14(11):2556. doi:10.3390/v14112556

what: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
text: Partitions observed growth advantage lineage two components: intrinsic transmissibility (fitness fully naive population) immune escape (additional fitness gained evading population immunity). decomposition follows framework Figgins Bedford (2025), effective fitness lineage \(v\) modelled \(f_v(t) = \beta_v \cdot (1 - \pi_v(t))\) \(\beta_v\) representing intrinsic transmissibility \(\pi_v(t)\) proportion population neutralising immunity \(v\).

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
code: fitness_decomposition(fit, landscape, generation_time)

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
text: fit lfq_fit object fit_model. landscape immune_landscape object immune_landscape. generation_time Generation time days (required converting growth rates reproduction numbers).

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
text: fitness_decomposition object (S3 class) components: decomposition Tibble columns: lineage, observed_advantage (total growth rate), beta (intrinsic transmissibility), escape_contribution (immune escape component), transmissibility_fraction (proportion due intrinsic fitness), escape_fraction (proportion due immune escape). fit input lfq_fit object. landscape input immune_landscape. generation_time Generation time used.

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition
text: decomposition proceeds follows. non-pivot lineage, observed growth rate \(\delta_v\) MLR fit taken total fitness difference relative reference. immune escape component estimated immunity differential: $$\text{escape}_v = -\log(1 - \bar{\pi}_v) + \log(1 - \bar{\pi}_{\text{ref}})$$ \(\bar{\pi}_v\) mean population immunity lineage \(v\) fitted period. intrinsic transmissibility component residual: $$\beta_v = \delta_v - \text{escape}_v$$ cross-immunity data available landscape object, effective immunity lineage computed weighted sum immunity sources, discounted cross-immunity matrix.

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition

title: Decompose variant fitness into transmissibility and immune escape — fitness_decomposition

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: Computes relative fitness lineage fitted model. Supports four output formats different use cases.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
code: growth_advantage(   fit,   type = c("growth_rate", "relative_Rt", "selection_coefficient", "doubling_time"),   generation_time = NULL,   ci_level = NULL )

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: fit lfq_fit object returned fit_model(). type Output type: "growth_rate" (default): raw growth rate delta per time_scale days (typically per week). "relative_Rt": relative effective reproduction number. Requires generation_time. "selection_coefficient": relative Rt minus 1. Requires generation_time. "doubling_time": days frequency ratio vs pivot double (positive = growing) halve (negative = declining). generation_time Mean generation time days. Required type = "relative_Rt" "selection_coefficient". Common values: SARS-CoV-2 approximately 5 days, influenza approximately 3 days. ci_level Confidence level intervals. Default uses level fitted model.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: tibble columns: lineage Lineage name. estimate Point estimate. lower Lower confidence bound. upper Upper confidence bound. type Type estimate. pivot Name pivot (reference) lineage.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: "relative_Rt" "selection_coefficient" types use Piantham approximation (Piantham et al. 2022, doi:10.3390/v14112556 ), assumes: Variants exponential growth phase (saturating due population-level immunity). variants share generation time distribution. Growth advantage reflects transmissibility differences; immune escape separately identified. Confidence intervals "relative_Rt" computed log-space (Wald intervals log-Rt), accurate linear delta method ratios.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: Piantham C, Linton NM, Nishiura H (2022). Predicting trajectory replacements SARS-CoV-2 variants using relative reproduction numbers. Viruses, 14(11):2556. doi:10.3390/v14112556 Abousamra E, Figgins M, Bedford T (2024). Fitness models provide accurate short-term forecasts SARS-CoV-2 variant frequency. PLoS Computational Biology, 20(9):e1012443. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012443

title: Construct a population immunity landscape — immune_landscape
text: data data frame columns date, lineage, immunity level. date Column name (unquoted string) containing dates. lineage Column name containing lineage/variant identifiers. immunity Column name containing population-level immunity estimates (proportion, 0–1 scale) lineage. type Character vector specifying immunity source(s): "infection", "vaccine", "hybrid", "combined" (default). data contain type column, per-source estimates preserved; otherwise estimates treated combined. cross_immunity Optional numeric matrix cross-immunity lineages. Rows columns correspond lineages; entry \((,j)\) degree immunity lineage \(\) protects lineage \(j\) (0 1). Default NULL assumes cross-protection data.

title: Construct a population immunity landscape — immune_landscape
text: immune_landscape object (S3 class) components: estimates Tibble columns date, lineage, immunity, optionally type. lineages Character vector lineage names. date_range Two-element Date vector. cross_immunity Matrix NULL.

title: Construct a population immunity landscape — immune_landscape
text: Immunity estimates may come multiple sources. Seroprevalence surveys provide direct measurements infrequent geographically sparse. Vaccination coverage data widely available capture waning variant-specific escape. Model-based reconstructions (e.g., case death data) can fill gaps introduce model dependence. immune_landscape() accepts input. critical requirement immunity expressed 0–1 scale representing proportion population neutralising protection lineage time point.

text: data data frame containing minimum columns lineage identity, date, count. lineage <tidy-select> Column containing lineage/variant identifiers (character factor). date <tidy-select> Column containing collection dates (Date class parseable character). count <tidy-select> Column containing sequence counts (non-negative integers). total <tidy-select> Optional column total sequences per date-location. title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
text: Performs following validation processing: Checks required columns exist correct types. Coerces character dates Date via ISO 8601 parsing. Ensures counts non-negative integers. Replaces NA counts 0 (warning). Aggregates duplicate lineage-date rows summing (warning). Computes per-time-point totals frequencies. Flags time points min_total unreliable. Sorts date ascending, lineage alphabetically.

Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
text: Applies post-hoc recalibration improve coverage properties prediction intervals forecast. Two methods available: isotonic regression (nonparametric, monotonicity- preserving) Platt scaling (logistic, parametric).

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
code: recalibrate(forecast_obj, bt, method = c("isotonic", "platt"))

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
text: forecast_obj lfq_forecast object recalibrate. bt lfq_backtest object providing calibration data. mapping nominal empirical coverage learned backtest residuals. method Recalibration method: "isotonic" (default) isotonic regression empirical coverage function, "platt" Platt scaling via logistic regression.

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
text: lfq_forecast object recalibrated .lower .upper bounds. object retains attributes original forecast can passed autoplot.

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
text: Isotonic regression: Learns monotone mapping nominal coverage levels observed coverage using backtest data, inverts find nominal level achieves desired empirical coverage. nonparametric approach requires distributional assumptions. Platt scaling: Fits logistic regression form \(P(\text{covered}) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\cdot z + b)\) \(z\) standardised residual, uses fitted model adjust prediction interval widths. methods require backtest object sufficient data estimate calibration mapping reliably (least 30 forecast-observation pairs recommended).

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
text: Platt JC (1999). Probabilistic outputs support vector machines comparisons regularized likelihood methods. Advances Large Margin Classifiers, 61–74.

title: Recalibrate prediction intervals — recalibrate
code: # \donttest{ sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8),   n_timepoints = 20, seed = 1) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr") fc  <- forecast(fit, horizon = 21) bt  <- backtest(sim, engines = "mlr",   horizons = c(7, 14), min_train = 42) fc_recal <- recalibrate(fc, bt, method = "isotonic") # }

title: Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
text: bt lfq_backtest object backtest(). metrics Character vector metrics compute: "mae": Mean absolute error frequency. "rmse": Root mean squared error. "coverage": Proportion within prediction intervals. "wis": Simplified weighted interval score single prediction interval stored backtest (typically 95%). "crps": Continuous Ranked Probability Score, assuming Gaussian forecast distribution (Gneiting Raftery, 2007). "log_score": Logarithmic scoring rule evaluated observed value Gaussian forecast density. "dss": Dawid-Sebastiani Score, proper scoring rule based predictive mean variance. "calibration": Mean squared calibration error across nominal coverage levels 10\

what: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
title: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
text: Computes population-level selective pressure metric genomic surveillance data alone, without requiring case counts epidemiological data. metric quantifies rapidly variant landscape shifting serves early warning signal epidemic growth robust case underreporting.

code: selective_pressure(fit, method = c("mean", "variance"))

title: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
text: fit lfq_fit object fit_model. method Aggregation method: "mean" (default) frequency-weighted mean growth rate, "variance" frequency-weighted variance growth rates (higher variance indicates stronger selection).

title: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
text: tibble columns: date Date. pressure Selective pressure value. dominant_lineage Lineage highest frequency. dominant_freq Frequency dominant lineage.

title: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
text: approach follows framework Figgins Bedford (2025), selective pressure defined variance-weighted mean growth rate across circulating lineages: $$S(t) = \sum_v p_v(t) \cdot \delta_v$$ represents expected rate population-average fitness increasing, measured entirely sequence data. method = "mean", metric positive fitter- -average lineages increasing frequency, indicating population-level adaptation. sustained positive value precedes epidemic growth means effective reproduction number average circulating virus increasing. method = "variance", metric captures heterogeneity fitness across co-circulating lineages. High variance indicates strong directional selection; low variance indicates near-neutral drift. metric requires genomic surveillance data. require case counts, hospitalisations, wastewater data, making applicable settings epidemiological reporting incomplete delayed.

title: Population-level selective pressure from variant dynamics — selective_pressure
text: Figgins MD, Bedford T (2025). Jointly modeling variant frequencies case counts estimate relative variant severity. medRxiv. doi:10.1101/2024.12.02.24318334

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: Generates synthetic lineage frequency data multinomial sampling model configurable growth advantages. Useful model validation, power analysis, teaching.

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
code: simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 4L,   n_timepoints = 20L,   total_per_tp = 500L,   advantages = NULL,   reference_name = "ref",   start_date = Sys.Date(),   interval = 7L,   overdispersion = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: n_lineages Number lineages including reference. Default 4. n_timepoints Number time points. Default 20. total_per_tp Sequences per time point. single integer (constant across time) vector length n_timepoints (variable sampling effort). Default 500. advantages Named numeric vector per-week multiplicative growth advantages non-reference lineages. Length must equal n_lineages - 1. Values > 1 mean growing faster reference, < 1 means declining. NULL, random values (0.8, 1.5). reference_name Name reference lineage. Default "ref". start_date Start date time series. Default today. interval Days consecutive time points. Default 7 (weekly data). overdispersion NULL (default), standard multinomial sampling. positive number, Dirichlet-Multinomial sampling concentration = 1 / overdispersion. Larger values = overdispersion. seed Random seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: lfq_data object additional true_freq column containing true (pre-sampling) frequencies.

what: Expected Value of Information for genomic surveillance — surveillance_value
title: Expected Value of Information for genomic surveillance — surveillance_value
text: Quantifies marginal decision value sequencing additional samples region stratum, based current posterior uncertainty variant frequency estimates. EVOI captures much expected estimation error decreases per additional sequence, enabling cost-effective resource allocation.

text: fit lfq_fit object fit_model. n_current Integer vector current sample sizes per stratum. single integer, assumed equal across lineages. n_additional Integer vector candidate additional sample sizes evaluate. Default seq(10, 200, = 10). target_lineage Optional character; compute EVOI specifically lineage. Default NULL evaluates across non-pivot lineages.

title: Expected Value of Information for genomic surveillance — surveillance_value
text: evoi S3 class components: values Tibble n_additional, evoi, marginal_evoi columns. current_uncertainty Numeric; current estimation variance (sum growth rate SEs squared). target_lineage Character NULL.

title: Expected Value of Information for genomic surveillance — surveillance_value
text: EVOI computed expected reduction mean squared estimation error lineage frequency \(n\) additional sequences observed. multinomial likelihood Gaussian approximation posterior, variance frequency estimate scales \(p(1-p) / n\), additional samples reduce factor \(n_0 / (n_0 + n_{\text{add}})\). marginal EVOI (value one additional sequence) derivative EVOI curve. decreases monotonically sample size, exhibiting diminishing returns characteristic information-theoretic quantities.
